title: OpenClaw rate limiter uses shared Redis key across tenants causing cross-tenant throttling

source.license_note: Synthetic ops scenario derived from openclaw/openclaw issue #62571.

task.summary: Diagnose why low-volume tenants are hitting rate limits that should only apply to high-volume tenants.
task.instructions: Use the incident evidence to confirm the rate limit key is shared across tenants, localize the fault to the key construction logic missing a tenant identifier, and submit a repair conclusion through the ops workflow.
task.acceptance_notes: This is an ops-only synthetic incident derived from a real GitHub issue.

visible_context.issue_text: Tenant B (low volume) begins receiving 429 Too Many Requests errors during peak usage by Tenant A. Both tenants share a plan tier but should have independent rate limit buckets.
reproduction_steps: Inspect the rate limiter key format and confirm tenant_id is absent.
Confirm the Redis counter is shared across all tenants on the same plan.
Decide whether the fault has been detected, localized, and repaired.
visible_context.notes: The expected repair scopes the rate limit key to include tenant_id (e.g. rl:tenant:{id}:plan:pro:endpoint:/v1/chat).

ops_fault.description: Synthetic incident where a missing tenant_id in the rate limiter key causes cross-tenant throttling.

constraints.notes: Ops-only smoke scenario. The standalone executor remains synthetic.

notes: Synthetic ops scenario derived from openclaw/openclaw issue #62571.
